results.title: Customer Complaints That Make No Sense — 49 Service Desk Stories
results.content: broken," he announced. "It gave me a reading of 98.6 this morning, and just now it says 99.1. Which one is it? I need a thermometer that can make up its mind and stick to one story." The store employee, trying to suppress a laugh, had to gently explain the basic biological concept that human body temperature naturally fluctuates throughout the day. The customer looked skeptical as if he'd just been told the sky wasn't really blue. 41. Some people struggle with bills. Others believe bills should be emotionally intelligent. One customer was furious that their phone bill arrived before they were emotionally ready to deal with it. It's due on the 15th, but you send it on the 5th, they demanded. That's 10 days of financial stress I didn't ask for. Their proposed solution was for corporations [...] The dictionary. 15. A customer was grappling with a serious technological paradox. Their brand new Bluetooth speaker refused to play music entirely on its own. It says wireless right on the box, they complained, but apparently that doesn't mean it's independent. Utterly frustrated by the concept of having to connect a phone or a laptop, they suggested that all speakers should come with built-in radios that just know what you want to hear. Because the monumental effort of tapping a screen a few times to play a song is for some simply a bridge too far in this modern age. 16. And now for a complaint that defies all logic. A customer called in to complain that the company's hold music was too effective. I was absolutely furious when I called. they explained. But after 5 minutes of your [...] wasted my time calling. He was essentially complaining that the company didn't give him enough time to wallow in his own inconvenience. He didn't want a solution. He wanted a struggle. 29. A formal complaint was submitted to a toy company regarding their popular toddler-sized plastic car. The primary issue cited it was not road legal. The frustrated father argued that the packaging never explicitly stated the toy car could not be driven in actual traffic. His disappointment apparently peaked when he was pulled over by a police officer, forcing him to explain that the vehicle in the fast lane was in fact just a Fisherpric product with a top speed of 2 mph. One can only imagine the officer's report on that incident. 30. Excuse me, I'd like to buy some Wi-Fi, a customer confidently announced

results.title: Online reviews are broken – here’s how to fix them
results.content:
Google and other feedback and reputation service providers need to invest more resources into the prevention, detection and removal of fake reviews. Machine learning technologies have made impressive leaps in recent years and could help in weeding out fake content.

Tighter rules governing the selection of reviewers enabling their participation under specific conditions. We’ve seen this with verified buyer schemes that aim to provide assurances that the reviewer has had a genuine experience with the business. [...] The presentation of the feedback and particularly the star scoring system could also have more contextual information, say through additional colour coding to communicate the sentiment mined out of the textual comments. In this case, highly emotional comments based on less factual or useful information could have a different colour from those trying to be impartial and objective.

Businesses also need to embrace the system for reporting problem reviews and use it responsibly. They should not report negative feedback if it is genuine, as this affects the relationship with the feedback platform, which will understandably be more distrustful to the business. [...] It’s surprising this sort of thing hasn’t emerged before. An over-reliance on the “wisdom of the crowd”, whereby many people measure things by the approval of the rest of the community, leaves us vulnerable to this kind of fraud.

It’s all about numbers. Products and companies are measured online by the number of stars they get on a five-star scale, influencers are measured by numbers of followers, posts are measured by the numbers of likes or retweets. The satirical Kardashian index provides a quantitative measure for academics by comparing citations of their research papers with their number of Twitter followers.
